drop_reason: Not a coding task. instruction.md is a single line ('Great, now save this haiku into entire_haiku.txt') and the canonical session deliverable is a 3-line free-verse poem written to entire_haiku.txt followed by `git add && commit && push`. No marin source code is touched — the cloned repo is incidental scratch space for a file write. install_config.json has FAIL_TO_PASS=[] PASS_TO_PASS=[] test_cmd='pytest -v' on the full marin repo at base commit d21ff56c, so the verifier falls back to overall pass-rate of unrelated upstream tests that the agent's haiku.txt cannot influence in either direction; score is dominated by repo-baseline noise, completely decoupled from the deliverable. The legacy test.sh.bak (5 weighted P2P gates: 3-line count, 5-7-5 syllable pattern, 'entire' keyword grep, anti-stub, line-length) is poetry grading, not coding evaluation, and additionally has a bug (`existing` referenced before assignment at line 227). No recoverable behavioral signal exists: the canonical patch is +3 lines of text with no code under test, and the original session has no debugging/implementation arc. Fails the 'outputs reconstructible from repo diff' criterion in spirit and the multi-turn-gap criterion (T0 ≈ 1.0 for any capable model on 'write 3 lines to a file' — zero correction-loop signal).
notes: Confirms prior batch DROP. Source session 0cd9a25d shows a developer using Claude as a generic file-write+git helper inside a marin-community/marin checkout — the repo is the workspace, not the subject. Even restoring the legacy weighted-P2P verifier (Σ weights = 1.00) only measures whether the agent produced a competent haiku, which is creative writing, not code repair under iterative user correction. No salvage path; recommend deleting harbor_tasks/marin-task-0cd9a2/ entirely.
